items.q: What is the difference between simple and compound interest?
items.correct: Simple interest is calculated only on the original amount; compound interest is calculated on the original amount plus accumulated interest.
items.misconception: Simple and compound interest give the same total — compound just uses a different formula.
items.random_wrong: Simple interest grows faster than compound because it has fewer steps.
items.why: Students don't appreciate that compound interest reinvests each period's interest, leading to exponential rather than linear growth.

items.q: What is the sum of the interior angles of any pentagon?
items.misconception: 360 degrees, because all polygons' angles sum to one full turn.
items.random_wrong: 180 degrees, the same as a triangle.
items.why: Students remember a single 'angles add up to' figure and apply it universally, or confuse interior with exterior angles (which do sum to 360).

items.correct: The square root of x (the positive root when x is positive).
items.misconception: x^(1/2) means x divided by 2 — half of x.
items.random_wrong: x^(1/2) means 1 divided by x squared.
items.why: Fraction-as-division intuition takes over; students don't connect fractional exponents to roots.

items.q: Using Newton-Raphson on f(x) = x² − 2 with x₀ = 1.5, what is x₁? (Give to 3 dp.)
items.why: It's 1.417. “1.500” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The trapezium rule estimates which kind of quantity?
items.why: It's a definite integral. “a derivative” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Use trapezium rule with 2 strips to estimate ∫₀² x² dx (h = 1).
items.why: Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
